picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.portfolioRole: Custom AI silicon and networking compounder
picks.thesisSummary: Broadcom combines accelerating AI semiconductor revenue with a fresh OpenAI processor announcement, giving today’s list a high-conviction custom-silicon and networking entry that is still in a pullback rather than a euphoric breakout.
picks.whyNow: Broadcom’s June 24 Jalapeño processor announcement with OpenAI followed a June 3 quarter in which AI semiconductor revenue beat and management guided another sharp step-up in fiscal Q3, giving the market a fresh product proof point plus a near-term revenue bridge.
picks.valuationContext: Broadcom is expensive on simple multiples, but the current setup remains more defensible than many AI peers because the revenue acceleration is explicit and free-cash-flow generation is still substantial.
catalysts: Broadcom and OpenAI said on June 24 that they unveiled Jalapeño, an intelligence processor built for current and future LLM inference workloads.
Broadcom reported June-quarter AI semiconductor revenue of $10.8 billion and said it expected fiscal Q3 AI semiconductor revenue of $16.0 billion.
risks: AI customer concentration: A meaningful share of upside still depends on a concentrated set of hyperscaler and model-builder AI programs.
Broadcom’s AI upside remains sensitive to a handful of very large customers and program ramps.
watchItems: Jalapeño commercialization: Watch for partner deployment details, broader customer references, and production-scale performance claims.
Fiscal Q3 AI revenue follow-through: The next report needs to validate management’s $16.0B AI semiconductor revenue outlook for fiscal Q3.
Change of view: If custom AI programs slip, networking demand cools, or the OpenAI processor does not translate into broader deployment momentum by the next earnings update, the score should be cut.

picks.name: Micron Technology, Inc.
picks.thesisSummary: Micron’s record June-quarter results and its Anthropic agreement make it one of the clearest why-now semiconductor opportunities on the board, even after a sharp move.
picks.whyNow: Micron reported record fiscal Q3 results on June 24 and paired that with a strategic Anthropic agreement announced June 22, making the memory upcycle look more structural and more directly tied to frontier AI infrastructure demand.
picks.valuationContext: The stock is no longer cheap after a major run, but the current setup still looks attractive relative to the scale of earnings revision and free-cash-flow expansion now visible in the business.
catalysts: Micron said on June 24 that fiscal Q3 2026 revenue reached a record and that adjusted free cash flow was $18.3 billion, with a quarterly dividend declared for July 21.
Micron said on June 22 that it entered a strategic agreement with Anthropic spanning AI memory and storage architecture design, supply, and enterprise adoption of Claude.
risks: Memory cycle reversal risk: Micron still operates in a cyclical industry where pricing and supply conditions can turn quickly after a strong upcycle.
The stock has already moved sharply, so a still-good business can still correct if expectations outrun execution.
watchItems: Fiscal Q4 delivery: Micron now needs to convert record fiscal Q3 momentum into another clean quarterly beat and stable HBM supply execution.
Anthropic agreement monetization: Watch for signs that the Anthropic agreement expands design, supply, or software-adjacent relevance beyond a one-off supply pact.
Change of view: If HBM supply tightness eases faster than expected, pricing cracks, or fiscal Q4 guidance stops rising, Micron should drop from the top tier.

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.thesisSummary: Oracle is one of the cleaner fresh cloud-infrastructure winners still under-owned relative to the scale of its remaining performance obligations and IaaS growth.
picks.whyNow: Oracle’s June 10 Q4 and FY2026 results showed another step-change in cloud infrastructure demand, with IaaS revenue up 93% in Q4 and remaining performance obligations up to $638 billion.
picks.valuationContext: Oracle remains expensive versus its historical software multiple, but the revenue base is now moving fast enough to justify treating it more like a capacity-constrained infrastructure platform than a static legacy software vendor.
catalysts: Oracle said on June 10 that Q4 cloud infrastructure revenue rose 93% year over year and remaining performance obligations grew to $638 billion.
ORCL is listed for 2026-09-09 on Alpha Vantage’s earnings calendar.
risks: Capex and execution intensity: Oracle’s AI cloud growth requires sustained heavy investment and flawless execution on capacity expansion.
Aggressive infrastructure spending can pressure returns if demand timing slips.
watchItems: Backlog conversion pace: Watch whether the $638 billion RPO base converts into revenue without margin or balance-sheet strain.
Next earnings checkpoint: The September 9 earnings date is the next audit point for OCI growth, capex intensity, and RPO conversion.
Change of view: If backlog growth slows, capex outruns monetization, or the next report shows OCI growth decelerating abruptly, Oracle should move down the board.

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.thesisSummary: RTX combines a fresh June 26 missile contract with a still-open July NATO spending checkpoint, giving defense exposure a cleaner why-now than the more frequently repeated contractors.
picks.whyNow: Raytheon’s $1.1 billion AIM-9X contract on June 26 arrived just ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit, keeping defense production and allied replenishment firmly in focus.
picks.valuationContext: RTX offers a more grounded valuation than several aerospace peers while still participating in the current rearmament cycle.
catalysts: RTX said on June 26 that Raytheon received a $1.1 billion U.S. Navy contract to produce AIM-9X Block II missiles for U.S. and allied customers.
RTX is listed for 2026-07-28 on Alpha Vantage’s earnings calendar.
risks: Program execution and budget risk: Defense names still rely on procurement timing, production execution, and budget follow-through.
Budget noise or program delays can compress the rating even when the long-term defense thesis remains intact.
watchItems: NATO summit follow-through: Watch whether the July 7-8 Ankara summit produces additional defense-spending follow-through that supports replenishment programs.
July 28 earnings date: RTX’s next earnings report is the next company-specific checkpoint for backlog, defense margin, and aerospace delivery quality.
Change of view: If program execution slips, the NATO summit fails to reinforce spending momentum, or commercial aerospace offsets weaken meaningfully, RTX should be downgraded.

picks.name: GE Vernova Inc.
picks.thesisSummary: GE Vernova offers a fresh power-demand and grid-modernization setup with both June order flow and AI-enabled transmission software evidence.
picks.whyNow: GE Vernova’s June 23 Vietnam LNG equipment order and its June 9 GridOS launch keep the company directly exposed to the physical power buildout needed for electrification and AI-driven load growth.
picks.valuationContext: The valuation already reflects quality, but it still looks acceptable relative to the long-duration power generation and transmission opportunity set.
catalysts: GE Vernova said on June 23 that it would supply two 9HA.02 gas turbines and two H78 generators to EVN’s Quang Trach II LNG power plant.
GE Vernova said on June 9 that it launched GridOS for Transmission and released AI whitepapers on grid planning and autonomous grid-edge operations.
risks: Large-project execution risk: Grid and generation businesses can be lumpy, with revenue timing dependent on complex project execution.
Project timing can be uneven even when the long-term power-demand thesis is intact.
watchItems: July 22 earnings report: The next earnings release will test whether order momentum is converting into margin and backlog quality.
Grid software adoption: Watch for customer adoption signals around GridOS and AI-enabled transmission planning tools.
Change of view: If major power projects slip, grid software demand underwhelms, or margin execution weakens at the next report, the score should come down.

picks.name: Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.
picks.thesisSummary: AMD remains a credible second-source AI platform with a defined July event catalyst and fresh sovereign-AI investment evidence from June.
picks.whyNow: AMD’s June 8 commitment to invest up to £2 billion in UK AI infrastructure and its scheduled July 23 Advancing AI 2026 event keep the story fresh without relying on hype-only speculation.
picks.valuationContext: AMD’s valuation is still demanding, but the current candidate-pool percentile is more reasonable than several AI infrastructure peers and the multiple is no longer expanding on pure narrative alone.
catalysts: AMD said on June 8 that it planned to invest up to £2 billion over five years in UK AI innovation, research, and advanced computing partnerships.
AMD said on April 28 that it would host its flagship Advancing AI 2026 event on July 23, 2026 to share new AI ecosystem and platform updates.
risks: Competitive AI execution: AMD still needs to prove it can capture a large enough share of AI infrastructure spend against bigger incumbents.
AMD still has to convert ecosystem ambition into market-share gains that are durable enough to defend the valuation.
watchItems: Advancing AI 2026: Watch whether AMD’s July 23 event shows stronger customer deployment evidence and software ecosystem traction.
August 4 earnings report: The next quarterly report needs to show continued AI and data-center progress, not just event-driven messaging.
Change of view: If July’s event fails to show meaningful deployment momentum, or if AI accelerator traction stays too narrow relative to expectations, AMD should move lower.

picks.name: Trane Technologies plc
picks.thesisSummary: Trane gives today’s list a cleaner building-efficiency and climate-tech exposure, backed by a fresh AI lab launch and a June leadership move tied to scaling the next growth phase.
picks.whyNow: Trane’s May AI Lab launch and June 10 COO appointment both point to the same underlying setup: rising customer demand for AI-managed efficiency, resilience, and climate infrastructure.
picks.valuationContext: The stock is not cheap, but the multiple remains supportable given margin quality, durable service economics, and secular efficiency demand.
catalysts: Trane said on May 20 that it opened a BrainBox AI Trane Technologies AI Lab and showroom in Montréal to accelerate AI-driven building solutions.
Trane said on June 10 that Donny Simmons would become COO effective July 1, citing expanded scale and growing market opportunities.
risks: Cyclical commercial demand: Even high-quality building-efficiency names remain exposed to pauses in commercial spending and construction activity.
A softer nonresidential demand cycle can compress the multiple even if the long-term efficiency thesis stays intact.
watchItems: July 29 earnings report: The next report should confirm whether AI-enabled and efficiency demand is broad enough to sustain premium pricing.
AI Lab customer uptake: Watch for customer deployments or partnerships that show the Montréal AI Lab is becoming commercially meaningful.
Change of view: If commercial HVAC demand softens materially, AI-driven offerings remain niche, or July earnings show weaker order conversion, the thesis should weaken.

picks.name: Digital Realty Trust, Inc.
picks.thesisSummary: Digital Realty earns a slot as the freshest real-estate single name because its June 22 land and platform transactions directly expand hyperscale capacity optionality rather than relying only on lower-rate hope.
picks.whyNow: Digital Realty’s June 22 platform-growth transactions, including a large Kansas City land acquisition for hyperscale development, created a tangible why-now catalyst ahead of its July earnings date.
picks.valuationContext: The valuation is reasonable relative to high-quality data-center peers, especially if new land and platform investments convert into visible leasing and power-backed growth.
catalysts: Digital Realty said on June 22 that it planned transactions including a large Kansas City land acquisition to support hyperscale data-center development.
DLR is listed for 2026-07-23 on Alpha Vantage’s earnings calendar.
risks: Leverage and power availability: Data-center REITs depend on financing access, large capex commitments, and timely access to utility and power infrastructure.
Higher rates or utility bottlenecks can impair the timing of otherwise attractive data-center demand.
watchItems: July 23 earnings report: Watch leasing, funding, and development commentary for signs that the platform-growth strategy is converting into cash flow visibility.
Kansas City land monetization: Monitor whether acquired land and Teraco ownership expansion translate into visible leasing and return-on-capital confidence.
Change of view: If leasing momentum stalls, financing conditions worsen, or the new land and platform investments fail to translate into visible growth, this should fall back to watchlist status.

picks.name: HCA Healthcare, Inc.
picks.thesisSummary: HCA makes the final list as a steadier healthcare cash-flow name with resilient volume and operating cash generation, offering balance against the slate’s heavier AI and industrial tilt.
picks.whyNow: HCA’s April 24 first-quarter results showed revenue growth and a 22% jump in operating cash flow, while the July 24 earnings date offers the next clean checkpoint for volumes, margins, and labor costs.
picks.valuationContext: HCA still looks more reasonably valued than many healthcare quality compounders given its cash generation and buyback-friendly economics.
catalysts: HCA said on April 24 that first-quarter revenue increased 4.3% and cash flows from operating activities rose 22.0% year over year.
HCA is listed for 2026-07-24 on Alpha Vantage’s earnings calendar.
risks: Labor and reimbursement pressure: Hospital margins remain sensitive to staffing costs, payer mix, and reimbursement changes.
Hospital operators can rerate quickly if labor costs or reimbursement trends worsen.
watchItems: July 24 earnings date: The next report should confirm whether same-facility demand and cash generation remain sturdy enough to sustain the thesis.
Labor and payer-mix trends: Watch for any deterioration in staffing costs or reimbursement pressure that would undermine the margin profile.
Change of view: If admissions weaken, labor costs reaccelerate, or reimbursement pressure squeezes margins in the July report, HCA should move lower.

picks.name: Vanguard Real Estate ETF
picks.thesisSummary: VNQ earns the ETF slot as a rate-sensitive real-estate sleeve with visible July macro checkpoints and a cleaner role in a long-term DCA watchlist than repeating XLU again.
picks.whyNow: The July 2 employment report and July 14 CPI release are the next major U.S. macro checkpoints for rate-sensitive real-estate exposure, while VNQ offers a diversified way to express that reset without single-name property risk.
picks.valuationContext: The ETF’s yield and diversified REIT exposure look acceptable for a rate-sensitive ballast sleeve, especially after rates uncertainty kept the group from fully rerating.
catalysts: The July 2 employment report is the next rates-sensitive trigger for diversified REIT exposure.
The July 14 CPI release is the next inflation checkpoint for rate-sensitive real-estate exposure.
risks: Rate sensitivity: Broad REIT exposure remains highly sensitive to inflation, employment data, and financing conditions.
This is still a macro-sensitive sleeve; the July data can quickly move the timing case against it.
watchItems: July 2 employment report: A hotter labor print would likely pressure rate-sensitive real-estate sentiment again.
July 14 CPI release: The next inflation print is the key near-term read-through for real-estate duration sensitivity.
Change of view: If payrolls or inflation reaccelerate enough to reprice rates higher, or if property-fundamentals weaken materially, VNQ should move back to watchlist-only.
